audits.summary: Audit of the original Uniswap V1 AMM; no critical issues found, minor recommendations on reentrancy guards and input validation.

audits.scope: Uniswap V2 core and periphery contracts
audits.summary: V2 audit found no critical vulnerabilities; medium findings related to price manipulation edge cases and integer overflow in periphery.

audits.scope: Uniswap V2 core math and AMM logic
audits.summary: Focused mathematical review of V2 invariant and fee calculations; one medium finding on rounding behavior in edge cases.
V3 audit identified one high-severity issue related to tick manipulation under specific conditions (mitigated before deployment) and several medium findings in fee accounting.

audits.scope: Uniswap V3 core math (TickMath, FullMath, SqrtPriceMath)
audits.summary: Mathematical correctness review of V3 fixed-point arithmetic libraries; all findings addressed prior to mainnet launch.

audits.scope: Uniswap V3 core — informal security review
audits.summary: Independent review by samczsun prior to V3 launch; one medium finding on flash loan callback ordering resolved before deployment.

audits.scope: Uniswap V3 Permit2 and Universal Router
audits.summary: Audit of the Permit2 signature-based approval system and Universal Router; one high finding on signature replay in multi-chain contexts was remediated.
Second audit of Permit2 confirming the OpenZeppelin high finding was fixed; residual mediums relate to allowance expiry edge cases.

exploit_history.description: Fake Uniswap V3 LP NFT phishing campaign — not a protocol exploit; users were tricked into approving malicious contracts that drained wallets. The Uniswap protocol itself was not compromised.
Uniswap Universal Router front-end DNS hijack attempt reported; no confirmed on-chain funds lost from the core protocol. Classified as infrastructure/social-engineering vector.
exploit_history.post_mortem_url: NOT_AVAILABLE_FREE_TIER — no confirmed public post-mortem found on Rekt.news or DefiLlama hacks page for this specific incident

dependencies.notes: Uniswap V3 exposes on-chain TWAP oracles used by many third-party protocols. The TWAP itself is manipulation-resistant over longer windows but can be attacked with large capital over short windows on low-liquidity pools. Uniswap core does not depend on external oracles for its own AMM logic.

Uniswap governance voted to use Wormhole for cross-chain governance message passing (e.g., to deploy on BNB Chain). Wormhole suffered a $320M exploit in Feb 2022; its use as a governance bridge introduces a high-severity dependency risk for cross-chain Uniswap deployments.

Uniswap V3 is deployed on multiple L2s whose centralized sequencers could theoretically reorder transactions (MEV/sandwich risk). Sequencer downtime would halt trading on those deployments but not affect Ethereum mainnet.
Core V3 contracts are immutable on Ethereum L1; no admin keys or upgrade proxies. Risk is limited to Ethereum consensus-layer failures, which are considered systemic/low-probability.

rationale: Uniswap's core V1–V3 contracts are immutable with no admin keys, making them among the most battle-tested and trust-minimized DeFi protocols; the protocol has never suffered a direct smart-contract exploit despite holding tens of billions in TVL at peak. The primary residual risks are (1) the Wormhole cross-chain governance bridge dependency introduced in 2023, which carries inherited bridge risk, and (2) the social-engineering/phishing surface around Permit2 approvals, which has already caused ~$8M in user losses even though the protocol itself was not breached.
